nodes.label: Deepening Opportunity
nodes.rationale: Turning shallow modules into deep modules concentrates complexity behind simpler interfaces, improving testability and AI navigability.

nodes.label: Deletion Test
nodes.rationale: A module is usefully deep when deleting it would disperse complexity rather than concentrate it.

nodes.label: ADR-Aware Architecture Review
nodes.rationale: Existing architectural decisions should not be re-litigated unless observed friction is strong enough to justify reopening them.

nodes.label: Throwaway Prototype
nodes.rationale: A prototype exists to answer one design question quickly; only the validated answer should survive.

nodes.label: Prototype State Visibility
nodes.rationale: Rendering the complete relevant state after every action or variant switch makes mistaken assumptions observable.

nodes.label: Explicit Prototype Question
nodes.rationale: Writing the state-model question down prevents a prototype from efficiently answering the wrong question.

nodes.label: Portable Pure Logic Module
nodes.rationale: Separating pure domain logic from the throwaway terminal shell lets the validated logic move into production while the shell is deleted.

nodes.label: In-Memory Prototype State
nodes.rationale: Keeping prototype state in memory isolates the design question from production persistence concerns.

nodes.label: Radically Different UI Variants
nodes.rationale: Variants must disagree about structure, hierarchy, and primary affordance so comparison resolves a real design decision rather than cosmetic taste.

nodes.label: Existing Page Sub-Shape
nodes.rationale: Hosting variants inside a real page preserves authentic data, navigation, density, and constraints that expose design problems hidden by an isolated route.

nodes.label: Prototype Production Gate
nodes.rationale: Hiding the switcher in production prevents an accidentally merged prototype control from reaching users.

nodes.label: Primary-Source Research
nodes.rationale: Claims should be traced to the official documentation, source code, specification, or first-party API that owns them.

nodes.label: Background Research Agent
nodes.rationale: Delegating reading to a background agent lets the main agent continue useful work while sources are investigated.

nodes.label: Pull Requests as a Request Surface
nodes.rationale: External pull or merge requests can enter triage as feature requests, while collaborators' in-flight work should remain outside that queue.
nodes.norm_label: pull requests as a request surface

nodes.label: Prompt-Driven Setup
nodes.rationale: The setup explores existing conventions, explains each decision, and confirms a draft before writing so repository-specific choices are not guessed.

nodes.label: Glossary Vocabulary Discipline
nodes.rationale: Using the canonical domain term in issues, refactors, hypotheses, and tests prevents synonym drift across engineering work.

nodes.label: ADR Conflict Flagging
nodes.rationale: Outputs that contradict recorded decisions should surface the conflict instead of silently overriding it.

nodes.label: Shared GitHub Issue and PR Number Space
nodes.norm_label: shared github issue and pr number space

nodes.label: Separate GitLab Issue and MR Number Spaces
nodes.norm_label: separate gitlab issue and mr number spaces

nodes.label: Behavior Through Public Interfaces
nodes.rationale: Tests should specify observable capability and survive internal refactoring by avoiding implementation details.

nodes.label: Pre-Agreed Test Seams
nodes.rationale: Confirming public seams before testing concentrates effort on critical paths and complex logic instead of every internal edge case.

nodes.label: Vertical Slice TDD
nodes.rationale: One test and one minimal implementation per cycle lets each tracer bullet respond to what the prior cycle revealed.

nodes.label: Dependency Injection
nodes.rationale: Passing external dependencies into a module makes system boundaries replaceable in tests without mocking owned internals.

nodes.label: SDK-Style Boundary Interface
nodes.rationale: Specific operation methods give each mock one stable shape and avoid conditional logic in test setup.

nodes.label: Interface-Based Verification
nodes.rationale: Observing results through the same public interface callers use avoids coupling assertions to database or internal side channels.

nodes.label: Independent Expected Value
nodes.rationale: Expected values must come from a known literal, worked example, or specification rather than recomputing the implementation.

nodes.label: Canonical Learning Language
nodes.rationale: Choosing one term per concept and consistently reusing it compresses language and lowers the cost of understanding more advanced concepts.

nodes.label: Evidence-Gated Term Promotion
nodes.rationale: A term enters the glossary only after the learner can use it correctly, making the glossary a record of compressed understanding rather than material merely presented.

nodes.label: Decision-Grade Learning Record
nodes.rationale: Learning records preserve non-obvious lessons and prior knowledge that change what future sessions should teach, rather than logging session activity.

nodes.label: Evidence of Learning
nodes.rationale: Demonstrated understanding, disclosed prior depth, corrected misconceptions, or a mission shift are evidence strong enough to set a new teaching baseline.

nodes.label: Learning Record Supersession
nodes.rationale: Contradicted understanding is marked superseded rather than deleted so the evolution of the learner's mental model remains visible.

nodes.label: Concrete Learning Mission
nodes.rationale: A specific real-world outcome gives teaching decisions a practical compass; an abstract wish to understand a topic does not.

nodes.label: One Mission per Workspace
nodes.rationale: Unrelated learning goals need separate workspaces so one mission can coherently steer lessons and resources.

nodes.label: High-Trust Resources
nodes.rationale: Teaching claims should come from primary sources, recognized experts, peer-reviewed work, and well-moderated communities rather than unverified model memory or marketing.

nodes.label: Resource Gaps and Pruning
nodes.rationale: Explicit gaps drive future search, while removing shallow or off-mission sources keeps the collection small and trustworthy.

nodes.label: Mission-Grounded Teaching
nodes.rationale: Every lesson and resource choice should trace to the learner's concrete reason for acquiring the topic.

nodes.label: Reusable Lesson Assets
nodes.rationale: Shared stylesheets, widgets, simulators, and diagram helpers keep lessons visually coherent and avoid duplicating components.

nodes.label: Storage Strength
nodes.rationale: Long-term retention is the goal because smooth in-the-moment retrieval can create an illusion of mastery.

nodes.label: Desirable Difficulty
nodes.rationale: Retrieval practice, spacing, and interleaving make skill practice effortful enough to build durable storage strength.

nodes.label: Tight Feedback Loop
nodes.rationale: Immediate, ideally automatic feedback lets learners correct performance while the attempted skill is still active.

nodes.label: Community Wisdom
nodes.rationale: Real-world communities let the learner test skills against practitioners and acquire judgment unavailable from knowledge alone.

nodes.label: Prefactoring
nodes.rationale: Making the change easy before making the easy change can remove structural obstacles before vertical feature slices begin.

nodes.label: Tracer Bullet Issues
nodes.rationale: Each issue should deliver a narrow but complete and independently verifiable path through all integration layers.

nodes.label: Dependency-Ordered Issue Publication
nodes.rationale: Publishing blockers first yields real issue identifiers that downstream slices can reference.

nodes.label: Parent Issue Preservation
nodes.rationale: The decomposition publishes child issues without closing or modifying the source issue.

nodes.label: Auto-Fill to Auto-Submit to Auto-Close Cascade Rule
nodes.norm_label: auto-fill to auto-submit to auto-close cascade rule
